summary: Joel Spolsky challenges the conventional wisdom that code reuse is always good and reinventing the wheel is always bad. He draws on his experience at Microsoft, where the Excel team's refusal to take on external dependencies allowed them to ship on time with high-quality code. He extends the argument beyond software to business generally, arguing that outsourcing core functions leads to loss of control and competitive disadvantage. His conclusion is that core business functions should always be done in-house, while non-core functions can be safely outsourced.
key_points: The Excel team's 'find the dependencies and eliminate them' philosophy led to consistently on-time shipping and high-quality code, despite seeming wasteful
When you have a great team, external code is often lower quality and introduces risks you can't control — growing your own lavender matters when you're a great chef
The dotcom-era vision of fully virtual companies that outsource everything fails because the market pays for value added, and outsourcing critical functions means losing control
The rule is simple: if it's a core business competency, build it yourself; if it's not, outsource it
Companies like Amazon and Google gain competitive advantages precisely because they build core infrastructure themselves rather than using off-the-shelf solutions
money_quotes: Find the dependencies — and eliminate them.
When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.

When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market, because sometimes they don't have fresh lavender or they have old lavender which they pass off as fresh.
If it's a core business function — do it yourself, no matter what.

Two yuppies in a living room buying an e-commerce engine from company A and selling merchandise made by company B and warehoused and shipped by company C, with customer service from company D, isn't honestly adding much value.

If you outsource fulfillment, and your fulfillment partner has a different idea about what constitutes prompt delivery, your customers are not going to be happy, and there's nothing you can do about it.
The only exception to this rule, I suspect, is if your own people are more incompetent than everyone else, so whenever you try to do anything in house, it's botched up.
key_insight: The conventional wisdom that code reuse is always superior to building in-house is wrong — core business functions should always be built internally because dependencies sacrifice control, quality, and competitive advantage.
